title: How to Slice Onions for a Traditional Belgian Stoofvlees
meta_description: Learn the best way to slice onions for Belgian stew with step‑by‑step tips and the 2 PCS Onion Holder & Slicing Guide – safe, uniform cuts every time.
html:
When you’re ready to make a classic Belgian stoofvlees, the first thing you’ll notice is how the flavor hinges on sweet, evenly‑sliced onions. Knowing how to slice onions for Belgian stew not only speeds up the cooking process, it also prevents the dreaded onion‑burn that can make a hearty dish taste bitter. Below is a practical, no‑fluff guide that walks you through the preparation, from choosing the right onion to using the 2 PCS Onion Holder & Slicing Guide for consistent results.

Selecting the Right Onion

Stoofvlees traditionally calls for yellow onions because of their natural sweetness and ability to caramelize without turning mushy. Look for bulbs that feel firm, have dry, papery skins, and are free of sprouting greens. A medium‑sized onion (about 8‑10 oz) yields roughly one cup of sliced pieces – enough for a 4‑serving stew.

Preparing the Onion for Uniform Cuts

Before you reach for any knife, trim the ends and peel the outer layers. The key to safe, even slices is a stable base. This is where the Onion Holder & Slicing Guide (2 PCS) shines. The stainless‑steel prongs grip the onion firmly, keeping it steady while you slice. The device also works for tomatoes and potatoes, making it a versatile kitchen tool.

  1. Place the onion on a cutting board, root side down.
  2. Insert the stainless‑steel prongs into the top and bottom of the bulb, making sure the onion sits upright.
  3. Adjust the guide to your desired thickness – ¼ inch is ideal for stoofvlees.
  4. Slice with a chef’s knife, letting the guide do the work; the onion stays locked in place.

The result is a stack of uniform rings that cook evenly, releasing their sugars at the same rate.

Technique: The Classic Cross‑Cut Method

If you don’t have the holder on hand, you can still achieve consistent slices using a simple cross‑cut approach:

  • Cut the onion in half from top to root.
  • Lay each half flat, then make parallel cuts toward the root, leaving about ¼ inch at the base.
  • Turn the onion 90 degrees and slice across the first cuts. The result is a dice of uniform pieces.

For stoofvlees, however, you’ll want rings rather than dice. The holder’s guide eliminates the need for the second set of cuts, saving time and reducing knife fatigue.

Cooking the Sliced Onions Properly

Once you have your perfect rings, add them to a heavy‑bottomed Dutch oven over medium‑low heat. Toss in a splash of oil or butter, then let the onions sweat for 5‑7 minutes. The goal is a gentle caramelization – you want them soft and golden, not browned. Uniform slices ensure each piece reaches the same stage at the same time, preventing pockets of burnt onion that could dominate the stew’s flavor profile.

If you notice any pieces sticking, the stainless‑steel prongs of the Onion Holder are also a handy meat tenderizer, allowing you to gently press down without damaging the cookware.

Integrating the Onions into Stoofvlees

After the onions are lightly caramelized, add cubed beef, a splash of Belgian beer, and aromatics like thyme, bay leaf, and a touch of mustard. The onions will continue to break down, thickening the sauce and adding a subtle sweetness that balances the richness of the beef. Stir gently every few minutes; the uniform slices will dissolve evenly, creating a velvety texture that’s the hallmark of a proper stoofvlees.

Cleaning and Maintaining Your Slicing Tool

The Onion Holder & Slicing Guide is dishwasher‑safe, but a quick hand wash with warm, soapy water keeps the stainless steel looking like new. Dry the prongs thoroughly to prevent water spots. Because the tool also doubles as a meat tenderizer, keep it separate from raw meat when cleaning to avoid cross‑contamination.

FAQ

Can I use the Onion Holder for other vegetables?

Yes. The stainless‑steel prongs are designed to grip onions, tomatoes, and potatoes, making it a versatile slicer for any recipe that requires uniform cuts.

Do I need a special knife with the holder?

No special knife is required. A standard chef’s knife works perfectly. The holder’s guide controls the thickness, so the knife’s edge can focus on a smooth, even motion.

Is the holder safe for left‑handed users?

The design is symmetrical, so left‑ or right‑handed cooks can use it comfortably. Just position the prongs so the onion is stable, and slice in the direction that feels natural.
